trajectory_summary: Randy runs a clean, procedurally complete call on a warm, self-motivated customer — verify items are largely covered, bill is collected, appointment is booked. The strategic failures are not in execution but in leverage: a highly qualified customer with a workshop solar system, a specific garage install preference, a planned house-roof replacement, and genuine long-term interest never gets a bill flip, a curiosity hook, or a personalized close. The call books but leaves the appointment structurally thin — no emotional anchor, no wife included in the booking, and a time-constraint protocol deviation that introduces no-show risk.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment (Monday 10AM), so a 'booked' outcome tag would be accurate. However, the appointment is structurally fragile: the wife is on the title and was not included in the booking, the time-constraint protocol was handled with a hedge rather than a firm anchor, and no emotional commitment was built. The booking is real but the sit probability is lower than the outcome tag implies.

primary_coaching_focus.why: Jody confirms his wife is on the title at 02:40 and Randy acknowledges it without acting on it. The appointment is booked for Jody alone. Per protocols.spouse_decision, this is the single highest structural risk to the sit — a co-decision-maker excluded from the appointment is the SESSION20 failure mode the schema explicitly names. The fix is one sentence at booking and costs nothing; the miss costs the sit.

key_moments.quote: a workshop that I currently have a small solar system in.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jody volunteers that he has already self-installed a solar system on his workshop. This is the single strongest qualification signal on the call — a customer who has already committed money and effort to solar is not a skeptic to be converted; he is a buyer looking for the right path. Randy's response ('Okay. Awesome. Sounds good. Perfect.') treats this as a checkbox answer and moves immediately to address verification. The strategic read would be that Randy is deferring the depth conversation to the specialist — but there is no evidence of that intent, and no curiosity hook is planted to bridge to the appointment. The reactive read holds: Randy missed the anchor.

key_moments.quote: We're actually looking at wanting to install this on our garage.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jody volunteers a specific, considered install preference — the garage, not the house — and explains his reasoning across two turns (metal roof is more accessible; he wants to keep the house roof clean). This is a customer who has done project planning, not just idle browsing. Randy confirms feasibility ('not a problem at all') but does not anchor on the specificity. The appointment pitch later ('a specialist go over this information with you') is generic — it does not tie back to the garage design Jody has been thinking about. A curiosity hook here ('the specialist will actually build the design specifically for your garage setup — metal roof installs have some interesting options') would have personalized the close without scope creep.

key_moments.quote: I know at some point I'm gonna have to replace the roof on my house, but, I wanna keep keep it clean right now.
key_moments.stewart_read: Jody explicitly states he knows a house roof replacement is coming. The schema defines this as a cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned trigger with a specific bridging template and a 'high' revenue impact rating. Randy says 'Sure. Yeah. Not a problem.' and moves to the title question. This is a clean miss — the roofing cross-sell signal is stated plainly, the schema has a defined response, and Randy does not execute it. The reactive read holds without contest.

Jody confirms his wife is on the title. Randy's response — 'Okay. Yeah. No problem.' — acknowledges the fact and moves on. The schema is explicit: a spouse on the title must be included in the appointment. Randy never asks whether the wife can be on the Monday 10AM call. The appointment is booked for Jody alone. Per protocols.spouse_decision, this is a protocol violation, not a judgment call. The negative example in the schema (SESSION20, Andy excluded) maps directly to this moment.
